title: Horizons Live | Why US Henry Hub natural gas prices are set to rise | Wood Mackenzie
gist: Wood Mackenzie forecasts Henry Hub natural gas prices rising to near US$5/mmbtu in real terms by 2035 as AI data-centre power demand, record LNG export FIDs and depleting low-cost US acreage end more than a decade of cheap US gas, with global implications for LNG competitiveness.

themes.theme: Structural end of cheap US gas era

insights.claim: Wood Mackenzie forecasts a sustained rise in Henry Hub natural gas prices approaching US$5/mmbtu in real terms by 2035, ending over a decade of low US gas prices.
insights.speaker: Gavin Thompson
insights.so_what: A structural doubling-plus of US benchmark gas prices by 2035 changes the competitiveness calculus for US LNG exporters, gas-fired power generators, and industrial gas consumers; investors should stress-test US gas-linked assets against a higher long-run price floor.
insights.quote: Wood Mackenzie forecasts a sustained rise approaching US$5/mmbtu in real terms by 2035, as demand surges and the supply conditions that kept prices low for over a decade begin to erode.

insights.claim: AI-driven data centre demand, record LNG export FIDs, and maturing supply economics are converging to reshape the Henry Hub benchmark, according to Wood Mackenzie's Horizons report.
insights.speaker: Gavin Thompson
insights.so_what: Three simultaneous demand/supply shocks (AI power demand, LNG export growth, depleting acreage) are identified as compounding drivers, suggesting the price rise is not a single-factor, easily-reversible trend.

insights.claim: The best US gas acreage is depleting, raising the cost of new supply and underpinning the higher long-term Henry Hub price forecast.
insights.speaker: Gavin Thompson
insights.so_what: Rising marginal supply costs suggest upstream gas producers with premium acreage retain a durable cost advantage as the broader cost curve shifts higher.

insights.claim: Higher Henry Hub prices are expected to affect US LNG competitiveness and international buyers, with consequences felt well beyond US borders.
insights.speaker: Gavin Thompson
insights.so_what: International LNG buyers relying on US-linked Henry Hub-indexed contracts should anticipate rising landed costs, potentially weakening US LNG's price edge versus other supply sources.
insights.quote: The consequences will be felt well beyond US borders.
